title: Digital Storage Game
context: You are a digital media technician who needs to explain to clients why their image and audio files are so large — and why compressing them loses quality. Over 6 weeks you will master how computers store sound and colour data, learn to calculate file sizes, and then build your own Python game that demonstrates these concepts in an engaging way. The game concept is yours to choose — your creativity is the only limit.
subjectPrompt: Before you begin, briefly describe the game you are planning to build (one sentence is fine — you can change your mind later).

tasks.title: Colour Depth and Image Storage
tasks.question:
Computers store images as a grid of pixels. Each pixel stores a colour value in binary.

KEY TERMS:
— Colour depth (bit depth for images): number of bits used per pixel
— More bits per pixel = more possible colours = larger file
— Colour depth of 1 bit = 2 colours (black/white)
— Colour depth of 8 bits = 256 colours
— Colour depth of 24 bits = 16,777,216 colours (True Colour / RGB)

FILE SIZE FORMULA:
File size (bits) = image width × image height × colour depth
Then divide by 8 for bytes, 1024 for KB, 1024 again for MB.

Answer ALL of these:

1. An image is 100 × 100 pixels with a colour depth of 8 bits. Calculate the file size in bits, then in bytes.

2. A 24-bit colour image is 1920 × 1080 pixels. Calculate the file size in MB. Show your working.

3. Why does increasing colour depth increase file size?

4. A black and white (1-bit) image is 200 × 200 pixels. How many bytes is it?
tasks.markScheme: 1: 100×100×8 = 80,000 bits = 10,000 bytes — 1 mark for bits, 1 mark for bytes. 2: 1920×1080×24 = 49,766,400 bits ÷ 8 = 6,220,800 bytes ÷ 1024 = 6,075 KB ÷ 1024 ≈ 5.93 MB — award 1 mark for correct bits, 1 mark for correct MB (accept 5.9–6.0 MB). 3: More bits per pixel means more data stored per pixel, so the total file requires more storage — 1 mark for a clear explanation. 4: 200×200×1 = 40,000 bits ÷ 8 = 5,000 bytes — 1 mark.

tasks.title: Sound Storage
tasks.question:
Sound is stored digitally by sampling the sound wave at regular intervals.

KEY TERMS:
— Sample rate: how many samples taken per second (measured in Hz or kHz)
  e.g. CD quality = 44,100 Hz (44.1 kHz)
— Bit depth: how many bits used to store each sample
  e.g. CD quality = 16 bits per sample
— Higher sample rate + higher bit depth = better quality + larger file

FILE SIZE FORMULA (uncompressed):
File size (bits) = sample rate × bit depth × duration (seconds) × channels
(Mono = 1 channel, Stereo = 2 channels)

Answer ALL of these:

1. A 10-second mono audio clip is recorded at 44,100 Hz with a bit depth of 16. Calculate the file size in bits, then in KB. Show your working.

2. The same clip is recorded in stereo. How does the file size change? Show the new calculation.

3. What happens to sound quality if you REDUCE the sample rate? Why?

4. Why might a game developer choose a lower bit depth for background music compared to a key sound effect?
tasks.markScheme: 1: 44100×16×10×1 = 7,056,000 bits ÷ 8 = 882,000 bytes ÷ 1024 ≈ 861 KB — 1 mark for correct bits, 1 mark for correct KB (accept 860-862). 2: Multiply by 2 channels = 1,764,000 bytes ÷ 1024 ≈ 1,722 KB — 1 mark for doubled value. 3: Fewer samples captured per second means the wave shape is less accurately represented, leading to lower audio quality / loss of detail — 1 mark. 4: Valid reason e.g. background music is less critical to gameplay so lower quality is acceptable to save storage/memory — 1 mark. Award marks generously for clear understanding even if exact figures differ slightly.

tasks.title: File Size Calculations — Extended
tasks.question:
Practise your file size calculations with these more challenging problems. Show ALL working.

1. A game contains a 30-second stereo sound effect at 22,050 Hz, 8-bit. Calculate the file size in KB.

2. A game sprite sheet is 512 × 512 pixels at 24-bit colour. Calculate the file size in KB.

3. A game developer has 50 MB of storage left. They need to store:
   — A 2-minute stereo music track at 44,100 Hz, 16-bit
   — A 1024 × 768 background image at 24-bit colour
   Do they have enough space? Show all calculations and give a clear YES or NO answer.

4. Explain ONE way a developer could reduce the file size of the music track without changing its duration.
tasks.markScheme: 1: 22050×8×30×2 = 10,584,000 bits ÷ 8 = 1,323,000 bytes ÷ 1024 ≈ 1,292 KB — 1 mark. 2: 512×512×24 = 6,291,456 bits ÷ 8 = 786,432 bytes ÷ 1024 = 768 KB — 1 mark. 3: Music: 44100×16×120×2 = 169,344,000 bits ÷ 8 = 21,168,000 bytes ÷ 1024 ÷ 1024 ≈ 20.19 MB. Image: 1024×768×24 = 18,874,368 bits ÷ 8 ÷ 1024 ÷ 1024 ≈ 2.25 MB. Total ≈ 22.44 MB — YES, they have enough — 2 marks (1 for each correct calculation, 1 for correct conclusion). 4: Reduce sample rate OR reduce bit depth OR convert to mono OR use compression — 1 mark for any valid answer with brief explanation.

tasks.title: Plan Your Python Game
tasks.question:
Time to plan your game! Your Python game must demonstrate knowledge of either sound storage, colour storage, or both — but the game concept is entirely your choice.

Some ideas to spark inspiration (you do NOT have to use these):
— A quiz that asks players to calculate file sizes
— A game where players guess colour depth from image quality descriptions
— A 'spot the difference' text game comparing storage settings
— A challenge where players optimise a game's assets within a storage budget

Write your full game plan:

1. Game title and one-sentence description
2. What does the player DO each round? (gameplay loop)
3. How does the game demonstrate sound or colour storage concepts?
4. What Python features will you use? (e.g. loops, functions, dictionaries, random)
5. How does the player win, lose, or get a score?
tasks.markScheme: Award 1 mark each: (1) title and clear concept, (2) clear and playable gameplay loop described, (3) genuine and specific link to storage concepts (not just 'it's about sound'), (4) at least 3 valid Python features named, (5) clear scoring or win/lose condition. Reward ambitious and creative plans — do not penalise unusual ideas.

tasks.title: Build: Core Game Structure
tasks.question:
Open your online Python IDE and build the core structure of your game.

This week, build the skeleton that everything else will plug into:
✅ A welcome message and introduction explaining the game
✅ At least one function defined (e.g. def play_round():)
✅ A main game loop (while or for)
✅ A score variable initialised to 0
✅ At least one round of gameplay working end-to-end
   (even if it's simple — the point is it runs without errors)

Take a SCREENSHOT of your game running at least one round.

Paste your code below.
tasks.starterCode:
# Digital Storage Game
# Your name:
# Date:

def welcome():
    print('Welcome to [Your Game Name]!')
    print('[Brief game instructions here]')

def play_round():
    # Your round logic here
    pass

def main():
    welcome()
    score = 0
    rounds = 5
    # Your main loop here

main()
tasks.markScheme: Award 1 mark: welcome message present and runs. Award 2 marks: at least one function defined and called correctly (1 mark if defined but not called, or called but not defined). Award 1 mark: loop structure present (for/while). Award 1 mark: score variable initialised. Award 2 marks: at least one complete round runs without crashing — test by reading the code logic (2 marks if fully working, 1 mark if clearly intended but has a minor bug). Screenshot: if described as showing a running game, confirms the above.

tasks.title: Build: Storage Concepts in Gameplay
tasks.question:
This week, build the part of your game that directly demonstrates sound or colour storage knowledge.

This is the heart of your game — it must include at least ONE of:
— A calculation the player performs or the code performs (e.g. file size)
— Data about colour depth, sample rate, or bit depth used in the game logic
— Questions or challenges that require the player to apply storage knowledge

Requirements:
✅ Storage concept clearly embedded in gameplay (not just mentioned in a comment)
✅ Player input used and validated (handle wrong input gracefully)
✅ Correct/incorrect feedback given to the player
✅ Score updated based on performance

Take a SCREENSHOT showing your storage concept working in the game.
Paste your updated code.
tasks.markScheme: Award 2 marks: storage concept (colour depth, sample rate, bit depth, or file size calculation) genuinely embedded in gameplay logic. Award 1 mark if only superficially referenced. Award 2 marks: player input taken and invalid input handled (e.g. try/except or .isdigit() check) — 1 mark if input taken but not validated. Award 2 marks: correct/wrong feedback given with score updated correctly. Award 1 mark if feedback present but score not updating. Award 1 mark: screenshot described showing storage concept in action.

tasks.title: Build: Multiple Rounds and Difficulty
tasks.question:
Extend your game to be more complete and replayable.

Add ALL of these:
✅ At least 5 different rounds or questions (no repeats)
✅ Some form of difficulty variation (e.g. harder calculations, less time-obvious answers, higher values)
✅ A lives system OR a timer OR a strike system (3 wrong = game over)
✅ Final score displayed as X out of Y with a rating message

Take a SCREENSHOT showing at least 3 rounds of your game running.
Paste your updated code.
tasks.markScheme: Award 1 mark: at least 5 distinct rounds or questions present in code. Award 1 mark: evidence of difficulty variation (different values, harder concepts, progression). Award 2 marks: lives/timer/strike system implemented and working correctly (1 mark if present but not functioning). Award 1 mark: final score shown as fraction. Award 1 mark: rating message varies based on score. Screenshot: confirms the above — award marks if described.

tasks.title: Build: Functions and Code Quality
tasks.question:
At Year 9, code quality matters as well as functionality.

Refactor your game so that:
✅ At least 3 separate functions are defined, each doing one clear job
   (e.g. welcome(), play_round(), show_score(), game_over())
✅ Each function has a comment above it explaining what it does
✅ No section of code is repeated — if you do something twice, it's in a function
✅ Variables have clear, meaningful names (not a, b, x)
✅ The main() function calls the others in logical order

Paste your refactored code. You do NOT need a screenshot this week — the code speaks for itself.
tasks.markScheme: Award 2 marks: at least 3 functions defined, each with a single clear purpose (1 mark if only 2 functions, 0 if only one or none). Award 1 mark: each function has a comment explaining its purpose. Award 1 mark: no obvious code repetition — same logic not copy-pasted. Award 1 mark: all variable names are meaningful (reject single letters for important variables). Award 1 mark: main() function orchestrates the others in logical order. This task rewards good habits — be generous if the code is well-structured even if imperfect.

tasks.title: Final Build: Complete and Test
tasks.question:
Final session. Complete your Digital Storage Game and test it fully.

Final checklist:
✅ Game runs from start to finish without crashing
✅ All rounds work correctly
✅ Score is tracked and displayed
✅ Storage concept is clearly demonstrated in gameplay
✅ Code is commented and well-structured (functions used)
✅ Game handles unexpected input without crashing

Take a FINAL SCREENSHOT of your complete game running.

Paste your final complete code.

Paste your Trinket or Replit share link.
tasks.markScheme: Award 1 mark: share link provided (or clear reason why not). Award 1 mark: final screenshot described showing complete game run. Award 1 mark: all rounds functional in code. Award 1 mark: score tracked and displayed. Award 1 mark: storage concept demonstrably present in game logic. Award 1 mark: input error handling present somewhere in code. Read the full code holistically — a complete, working, well-structured game should score full marks.

tasks.title: Evaluation
tasks.question:
Write a technical evaluation of your Digital Storage Game.

Answer ALL of these:

1. How does your game demonstrate sound or colour storage concepts? Give TWO specific examples from your code or gameplay.

2. A client asks: 'Why is my uncompressed 24-bit colour image so much larger than my old 8-bit one at the same resolution?' Write a clear technical answer (3-4 sentences) as if you are explaining it to them.

3. Identify ONE part of your Python code you are most proud of and explain why it was technically challenging.

4. If you were to develop this game further, describe ONE feature that would deepen the player's understanding of digital storage. Explain how it would work.

5. Reflect honestly: what would you do differently if you started this project again?
tasks.markScheme: Award 1 mark: two specific examples of storage concepts in gameplay (vague answers score 0). Award 1 mark: technically accurate explanation of colour depth and file size for the client (must mention bits per pixel and multiplication). Award 1 mark: specific code achievement identified with genuine technical explanation. Award 1 mark: realistic feature suggestion that genuinely deepens storage understanding. Award 1 mark: honest and specific reflection — 'nothing' or vague answers score 0. This is a Year 9 evaluation — expect and reward technical precision.

gradingInstructions: You are marking Year 9 Computer Science work. The student has been building a Python game about sound and colour storage over 6 weeks using an online IDE. Mark each task against its mark scheme. Be encouraging but appropriately rigorous for Year 9 — reward technical precision and penalise vague answers. For code tasks: reward correct logic and good structure; be forgiving of minor syntax errors if intent is clear. Screenshots cannot be viewed — award marks if the student describes what the screenshot shows. Overall feedback: 3-5 sentences. Acknowledge the ambition of their project, highlight their strongest technical achievement in either Python or storage theory, then give one specific and actionable improvement target appropriate for Year 9. Return ONLY valid JSON — no preamble, no markdown fences.

gradingOutputFormat.overallFeedback: string (3-5 sentences, rigorous but encouraging for Year 9)
